description:
You are an automation specialist who was asked to add a **very quick credential-leak check** to an existing build workflow.

    1. Under the directory “/home/user/projects/” there are several Bash scripts.  
       Your job is to look through **every “*.sh” file anywhere inside /home/user/projects (recursively)** and detect *plain-text password assignments*.  
       A *plain-text password assignment* for this task is defined as any line that, **after all leading whitespace is removed**, begins with one of the exact strings below (case-sensitive) followed immediately by an equals sign “=”:
       • `password`  
       • `db_password`  
       • `passwd`

    2. Produce a single text file named  
       /home/user/security_report.txt  
       that contains one line for every offending line you found.  
       Use *relative* paths with respect to “/home/user/projects”.  
       Each line in the report **must use the exact format**:

           <relative_path_to_file>:<line_number>:<matched_variable_name>=<value>

       Rules for the output line:
       • <relative_path_to_file>  → path from “/home/user/projects” to the file (do not start with “./”).  
       • <line_number>            → 1-based line number within that file where the match occurs.  
       • <matched_variable_name>  → one of the three keywords above exactly as it appeared.  
       • <value>                  → everything that appears to the right of the “=”, preserved verbatim, with no surrounding spaces trimmed.

       Example (for illustration only):  
           scripts/deploy.sh:12:db_password=secret123

    3. If *no* password assignments are found, the file /home/user/security_report.txt must contain the single line:  
           NO LEAKS FOUND

    4. Do **not** print the report contents to STDOUT; the automated grader will read the file itself.

    Hints (do NOT reveal real paths/contents):  
    • You will likely need only 2-3 shell commands (find/grep/awk/sed, etc.).  
    • Work entirely under your regular user; no escalation is required.
truth:
<!--
        Initial filesystem state that the tests rely on
    -->
    Directory layout that already exists when the agent starts:

    /home/user/projects
    ├── deploy.sh
    ├── README.md
    └── utils
        ├── cleanup.sh
        └── helper.sh

    Contents of the files (tabs are literal TAB characters):

    1. /home/user/projects/deploy.sh  
       --------------------------------
       #!/usr/bin/env bash
       # Deploy script
       db_password=secret123
       echo "Deploying application..."
       # END
       --------------------------------

    2. /home/user/projects/README.md  
       --------------------------------
       Project documentation goes here.
       --------------------------------

    3. /home/user/projects/utils/cleanup.sh  
       --------------------------------
       #!/usr/bin/env bash
        password =cleanme        # <- note the space before '='; should NOT be matched
       password=cleanme2
       echo "Cleanup completed"
       --------------------------------

    4. /home/user/projects/utils/helper.sh  
       --------------------------------
       #!/usr/bin/env bash
       echo "Helper"
       passwd=letmein
       --------------------------------

    Expected result file *after* successful agent execution:

    /home/user/security_report.txt  
    --------------------------------
    deploy.sh:3:db_password=secret123
    utils/cleanup.sh:3:password=cleanme2
    utils/helper.sh:4:passwd=letmein
    --------------------------------

    Notes for verifier:
    • Line numbers are 1-based and verified.
    • The blank‐spaced assignment in cleanup.sh line 2 (`password =cleanme`) must NOT appear.
    • Ordering of lines in the report does not matter; grader will sort before comparison.
name: task_000000_a07e40d7
